---
title: "Quest 17: Higher-Order Functions"
subtitle: "Pass Behavior into Reusable Functions"
format:
  live-html:
    code-tools: true
---

::: {.quest-badge}
🧠 QUEST 17 | Difficulty: Beginner | Time: 10 minutes
:::

::: {.tip-box}
**💻 Practice Options:**
- 🦀 [Open Rust Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/)
- 📓 [View Notebook](../files/lessons/17-higher-order-functions.ipynb)
- 📥 [Download Notebook](../files/lessons/17-higher-order-functions.ipynb)
:::

## 📖 Introduction
Higher-order functions accept functions/closures as inputs.
This pattern helps you write flexible, reusable logic.

## 💡 Example

```rust
fn apply_twice<F>(value: i32, f: F) -> i32
where
    F: Fn(i32) -> i32,
{
    f(f(value))
}

fn main() {
    let plus_two = |x| x + 2;
    println!("{}", apply_twice(5, plus_two));
}
```

## 🎯 Gentle Challenge
Create a closure that multiplies by 3 and pass it into `apply_twice`.
Then test a second closure with a different operation.

## ✅ One Possible Solution

```rust
fn apply_twice<F>(value: i32, f: F) -> i32
where
    F: Fn(i32) -> i32,
{
    f(f(value))
}

fn main() {
    let times_three = |x| x * 3;
    println!("{}", apply_twice(2, times_three));
}
```

::: {.quest-complete}
### ✨ Quest 17 Complete
You passed behavior as data using closures.
Next: [Quest 18: Wrappers](18-decorators.qmd)
:::
